title: Vectors and vectorization
question: What is a vector in R and why is vectorization preferred over explicit loops?
answer: A vector is R's fundamental data structure: a one-dimensional, ordered collection of elements that all share the same atomic type (logical, integer, double, character, or complex). Vectorization applies an operation to every element at once, delegating the loop to fast compiled C code instead of the R interpreter, which makes code both shorter and dramatically faster.
explanation: Because R was designed around vectors, most operators and functions are vectorized by default. Writing x + y adds the two vectors element-by-element without a visible for loop. Interpreted R loops carry per-iteration overhead, so vectorized expressions typically outperform them by orders of magnitude on large data while also being easier to read.
codeExample:
# Scalar loop (slow, verbose)
x <- 1:1e6
out <- numeric(length(x))
for (i in seq_along(x)) out[i] <- x[i]^2

# Vectorized (fast, idiomatic)
out <- x^2

# Element-wise ops recycle automatically
a <- c(1, 2, 3)
b <- c(10, 20, 30)
a + b        # 11 22 33
a * 2        # 2 4 6
bestPractices: Reach for vectorized operations before writing a for loop
Preallocate results if a loop is truly necessary
commonMistakes: Growing a vector inside a loop with c() on every iteration
interviewTips: Be ready to explain why vectorization is faster (compiled C vs interpreted loop)

title: Recycling rules
question: How does R's recycling rule work when operating on vectors of different lengths?
answer: When two vectors have different lengths, R recycles (repeats) the shorter one until it matches the length of the longer one, then performs the operation element-wise. If the longer length is not an exact multiple of the shorter length, R still recycles but emits a warning about the incomplete cycle.
explanation: Recycling is what lets expressions like x * 2 work: the scalar 2 is a length-1 vector recycled across all elements. It is powerful but can silently hide bugs when lengths are mismatched unintentionally, which is why the partial-recycling case produces a warning rather than an error.
codeExample:
c(1, 2, 3, 4) + c(10, 20)      # 11 22 13 24 (10,20 recycled)

c(1, 2, 3) * 2                 # 2 4 6 (scalar recycled)

# Length mismatch that is not a multiple -> warning
c(1, 2, 3) + c(10, 20)
# Warning: longer object length is not a
# multiple of shorter object length
bestPractices: Ensure vector lengths match intentionally
Treat recycling warnings as likely bugs
commonMistakes: Ignoring the 'longer object length' warning
interviewTips: Mention that scalars are just length-1 vectors that get recycled

title: Data frames explained
question: What is a data frame in R and how does it differ from a matrix?
answer: A data frame is a list of equal-length columns presented as a rectangular table, where each column can hold a different type (numeric, character, factor, etc.). A matrix, by contrast, is a single atomic vector with dimensions, so every element must share the same type. Data frames are the standard structure for tabular, heterogeneous datasets.
explanation: Under the hood a data frame is a list whose elements are the columns, which is why column-wise operations are efficient and why str() shows it as a list. Matrices are backed by one contiguous vector, making them ideal for linear-algebra and numeric-only work but unsuitable for mixed types.
codeExample:
df <- data.frame(
  name = c("Ada", "Alan"),
  age = c(36L, 41L),
  active = c(TRUE, FALSE),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

str(df)
nrow(df); ncol(df)
df$age            # extract a column
df[df$active, ]   # filter rows

# Matrix: single type only
m <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2)
bestPractices: Use data frames (or tibbles) for heterogeneous tabular data
Set stringsAsFactors = FALSE on older R versions
commonMistakes: Expecting a matrix to hold mixed types
interviewTips: Say a data frame is fundamentally a list of columns

title: Factors vs characters
question: What are factors in R and when should you use them instead of character vectors?
answer: A factor is a vector for categorical data stored as integer codes plus a set of labelled levels. Use factors when a variable has a fixed, known set of categories and especially when order matters (ordered factors) or when a modelling function needs categorical predictors. Use plain character vectors for free-form text or identifiers.
explanation: Factors save memory for repeated categories and let statistical models treat categories correctly, including controlling the reference level. However, their integer-backed nature causes classic surprises: converting a factor directly with as.numeric() returns the codes, not the labels, so you must go through as.character() first.
codeExample:
sizes <- factor(c("S", "L", "M", "S"),
                levels = c("S", "M", "L"),
                ordered = TRUE)
levels(sizes)      # "S" "M" "L"
sizes < "L"        # comparison works because ordered

# Pitfall: numeric factor -> codes, not values
f <- factor(c("10", "20", "30"))
as.numeric(f)                 # 1 2 3 (WRONG)
as.numeric(as.character(f))   # 10 20 30 (correct)
bestPractices: Set explicit levels to control ordering and reference category
Use ordered = TRUE for ordinal data
commonMistakes: Calling as.numeric() on a factor of numeric-looking strings
interviewTips: Explain the integer-codes-plus-levels internal representation

title: Lists in R
question: How does a list differ from an atomic vector in R?
answer: An atomic vector holds elements of a single type in a flat structure, whereas a list is a generic container whose elements can be of any type and any length, including other lists, functions, or data frames. This makes lists the go-to structure for heterogeneous or nested data.
explanation: Indexing differs importantly: single brackets [ ] return a sub-list, while double brackets [[ ]] extract the element itself. The $ operator is shorthand for [[ ]] by name. Many R structures, including data frames and model objects, are built on lists.
codeExample:
lst <- list(
  id = 1L,
  name = "Grace",
  scores = c(90, 85, 100),
  meta = list(active = TRUE)
)

lst["scores"]     # a list of length 1
lst[["scores"]]   # the numeric vector
lst$scores        # same as [[ ]]
lst$meta$active   # nested access

length(lst)       # 4
bestPractices: Use [[ ]] or $ to extract elements, [ ] to subset
Name list elements for readable access
commonMistakes: Confusing [ ] (sublist) with [[ ]] (element)
interviewTips: Highlight that data frames are specialized lists

title: The apply family
question: Compare apply, lapply, sapply, vapply, and mapply in R.
answer: apply iterates over the margins (rows/columns) of a matrix or array; lapply maps a function over a list/vector and always returns a list; sapply is like lapply but simplifies the result to a vector or matrix when possible; vapply is a safer sapply that requires you to declare the return type/length; and mapply is the multivariate version, applying a function over several vectors in parallel.
explanation: The family provides functional iteration without explicit loops. sapply is convenient but its output type can vary, which is dangerous in production code; vapply removes that ambiguity by validating each result against a template. mapply (and its wrapper Map) zips multiple inputs together element-wise.
codeExample:
m <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2)
apply(m, 1, sum)          # row sums -> 9 12
apply(m, 2, sum)          # column sums

lapply(1:3, function(x) x^2)          # list
sapply(1:3, function(x) x^2)          # 1 4 9
vapply(1:3, function(x) x^2, numeric(1))  # type-safe

mapply(function(a, b) a + b, 1:3, 4:6)    # 5 7 9
bestPractices: Prefer vapply in production for predictable output types
Use lapply when you truly want a list back
commonMistakes: Relying on sapply's simplification, which can silently change type
interviewTips: Stress that vapply is the type-safe version of sapply

title: Environments and scoping
question: How does lexical scoping work in R and what role do environments play?
answer: R uses lexical (static) scoping: a free variable inside a function is looked up in the environment where the function was defined, not where it is called. Environments are the mutable containers that hold name-to-value bindings; each function call creates a new environment whose parent is the function's defining environment, forming a lookup chain.
explanation: When R evaluates a symbol it searches the current environment, then walks up the chain of parent environments until it finds a binding or reaches the empty environment. This is the mechanism behind closures. Unlike most objects, environments have reference semantics, so they are not copied on assignment.
codeExample:
x <- 10
f <- function() {
  x <- 20
  g <- function() x   # captures x from f's environment
  g()
}
f()                    # 20 (lexical, not dynamic)

# Environments have reference semantics
e <- new.env()
e$count <- 0
bump <- function(env) env$count <- env$count + 1
bump(e); bump(e)
e$count                # 2
bestPractices: Rely on lexical scoping rather than global mutable state
Use environments deliberately when you need reference semantics
commonMistakes: Assuming dynamic scoping (lookup at call site)
interviewTips: Contrast lexical vs dynamic scoping with a concrete example

title: Functions and closures
question: What is a closure in R and how is it used to build function factories?
answer: A closure is a function bundled together with the environment in which it was created, so it can access variables from that enclosing scope even after the outer function has returned. This lets you write function factories: functions that return specialized functions carrying captured configuration.
explanation: Because R functions capture their defining environment, a returned inner function keeps a live reference to the outer call's environment and its variables. Every R closure created in a loop must be careful about lazy evaluation of arguments; using force() ensures the captured value is what you expect.
bestPractices: Use force() to capture arguments eagerly in factories
Use <<- deliberately for closure state
commonMistakes: Lazy evaluation capturing the wrong value in loops
interviewTips: Explain that a closure = function + its enclosing environment

title: NA and missing data
question: How does R represent missing values and how do you handle them correctly?
answer: R uses the special value NA to denote a missing value, with typed variants like NA_integer_ and NA_character_. NA is contagious: most arithmetic and comparisons involving NA return NA. To handle it you test with is.na(), pass na.rm = TRUE to aggregate functions, or drop rows with na.omit() or complete.cases().
explanation: Because NA means 'unknown', comparisons such as NA == NA correctly yield NA rather than TRUE, so you must never test equality against NA. Distinguish NA (missing) from NULL (absence of a value/zero length) and from NaN (undefined numeric result like 0/0).
codeExample:
x <- c(1, NA, 3, NA, 5)
mean(x)                 # NA
mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)   # 3

is.na(x)                # F T F T F
x[!is.na(x)]            # 1 3 5

x == NA                 # NA NA NA NA NA (wrong test!)

df <- data.frame(a = c(1, NA), b = c(NA, 4))
na.omit(df)             # rows with any NA removed
bestPractices: Always test missingness with is.na(), never == NA
Decide explicitly between na.rm and dropping rows
commonMistakes: Confusing NA, NULL, and NaN
interviewTips: Point out that NA == NA returns NA because missing is unknown

title: dplyr core verbs
question: What are the core dplyr verbs and what does each one do?
answer: dplyr provides a small grammar of data manipulation: filter() keeps rows matching conditions, select() picks columns, mutate() adds or changes columns, arrange() sorts rows, summarise() collapses groups into summary values, and group_by() defines the grouping for subsequent summaries. These verbs compose cleanly with the pipe.
explanation: Each verb takes a data frame (or tibble) as its first argument and returns a data frame, which is what makes them chainable. group_by() plus summarise() is the split-apply-combine pattern, letting you compute aggregates per group in one readable pipeline.
codeExample:
library(dplyr)

mtcars %>%
  filter(cyl >= 6) %>%
  mutate(power_to_weight = hp / wt) %>%
  group_by(cyl) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_mpg = mean(mpg),
    max_ptw = max(power_to_weight),
    n = n()
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_mpg))
bestPractices: Chain verbs with the pipe for readable pipelines
Use group_by() + summarise() for aggregations
commonMistakes: Forgetting to ungroup() after grouped operations
interviewTips: Frame the verbs as a 'grammar of data manipulation'

title: Pipes: %>% vs |>
question: What is the difference between the magrittr pipe %>% and the native pipe |> in R?
answer: %>% is the magrittr pipe from the tidyverse, while |> is the base-R native pipe added in R 4.1. Both pass the left-hand value into the right-hand function, but %>% is more flexible: it supports the . placeholder anywhere in the call, whereas the native |> only inserts into the first argument (with a limited _ placeholder in later versions).
explanation: The native pipe is lightweight and dependency-free, parsed at the syntax level, making it slightly faster and always available. %>% is a function call under the hood and offers extra features like . placeholders and implicit first-argument insertion into complex expressions, which matters for some tidyverse idioms.
codeExample:
library(magrittr)

# magrittr pipe with a . placeholder
c(1, 4, 9) %>% sqrt() %>% sum()          # 6
mtcars %>% lm(mpg ~ hp, data = .)        # . placeholder

# native pipe (R >= 4.1)
c(1, 4, 9) |> sqrt() |> sum()            # 6

# native placeholder (R >= 4.2)
mtcars |> lm(mpg ~ hp, data = _)
bestPractices: Prefer |> for base-only code, %>% when you need . placeholders
Keep pipelines readable with one verb per line
commonMistakes: Assuming |> supports the . placeholder like magrittr
interviewTips: Mention |> was introduced in R 4.1 and is parsed, not a function

title: S3 vs S4 vs R5/R6
question: Compare the S3, S4, and R5/R6 object-oriented systems in R.
answer: S3 is R's simple, informal OOP system using a class attribute and generic functions that dispatch on the first argument; it is flexible but unenforced. S4 is a formal system with declared classes, typed slots, and multiple dispatch, giving stronger guarantees at the cost of verbosity. R5 (Reference Classes) and the popular R6 package provide mutable, message-passing objects with reference semantics, closer to Java/Python classes.
explanation: S3 dispatch works by name convention: calling print(x) looks for print.<class>. S4 uses setClass/setGeneric/setMethod and validates slots. R6 objects are environments, so methods mutate self in place rather than returning copies, which suits stateful objects like connections or counters.
codeExample:
# S3
circle <- structure(list(r = 2), class = "circle")
area <- function(x) UseMethod("area")
area.circle <- function(x) pi * x$r^2
area(circle)

# S4
setClass("Circle", representation(r = "numeric"))
setGeneric("area2", function(obj) standardGeneric("area2"))
setMethod("area2", "Circle", function(obj) pi * obj@r^2)

# R6 (mutable, reference semantics)
library(R6)
Counter <- R6Class("Counter", public = list(
  n = 0,
  inc = function() { self$n <- self$n + 1; invisible(self) }
))
bestPractices: Use S3 for lightweight, idiomatic R code
Use R6 when you need mutable, stateful objects
commonMistakes: Mixing @ (S4 slots) with $ (S3/list) access
interviewTips: Summarize: S3 informal, S4 formal, R6 mutable/reference

title: purrr map functions
question: How do purrr's map functions improve on the base apply family?
answer: purrr's map() family provides consistent, type-stable functional iteration. map() always returns a list, while typed variants like map_dbl(), map_chr(), map_int(), and map_lgl() guarantee an atomic vector of that type, failing loudly if the output does not match. map2() and pmap() handle two or many parallel inputs, and formula shorthand (~ .x) keeps code concise.
explanation: The key advantage over sapply is predictability: the typed suffix documents and enforces the return type, preventing the silent simplification bugs sapply can introduce. purrr also offers ergonomic helpers like the ~ .x lambda syntax and map_dfr()/map_dfc() to bind results into a data frame.
codeExample:
library(purrr)

map(1:3, ~ .x^2)              # list(1, 4, 9)
map_dbl(1:3, ~ .x^2)         # 1 4 9 (type-stable)
map_chr(letters[1:3], toupper)  # "A" "B" "C"

map2_dbl(1:3, 4:6, `+`)      # 5 7 9

# Read many files into one data frame
# files |> map_dfr(readr::read_csv)
bestPractices: Use typed map_*() variants for predictable output
Use map_dfr() to combine results into a data frame
commonMistakes: Using map() when you needed an atomic vector (map_dbl)
interviewTips: Contrast map_dbl's type safety with sapply's simplification

title: Matrix operations
question: How do you perform common matrix operations in R such as multiplication and transpose?
answer: R has first-class matrix support. Use %*% for true matrix multiplication (versus * for element-wise), t() to transpose, solve() to invert a matrix or solve a linear system, and functions like crossprod(), diag(), and det() for common linear-algebra tasks. Matrices are atomic vectors with a dim attribute.
explanation: Distinguishing %*% from * is essential: * multiplies element-wise with recycling, while %*% follows linear-algebra rules where inner dimensions must conform. solve(A, b) is the numerically preferred way to solve Ax = b rather than computing an explicit inverse.
codeExample:
A <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), nrow = 2)
B <- matrix(c(5, 6, 7, 8), nrow = 2)

A %*% B          # matrix multiplication
A * B            # element-wise (NOT matrix mult)
t(A)             # transpose
solve(A)         # inverse
det(A)           # determinant

# Solve Ax = b
b <- c(1, 1)
solve(A, b)      # preferred over solve(A) %*% b
bestPractices: Use %*% for matrix multiplication, * only for element-wise
Prefer solve(A, b) over solve(A) %*% b
commonMistakes: Using * expecting matrix multiplication
interviewTips: Clearly distinguish %*% from * in your answer

title: Reading data into R
question: How do you read CSV data into R and why prefer readr over base read.csv?
answer: Base R reads delimited files with read.csv()/read.table(), which return data frames. The tidyverse readr package offers read_csv(), which is faster, returns a tibble, does not convert strings to factors by default, keeps original column names, and reports a clear column-type specification. For large data, data.table::fread() is faster still.
explanation: read_csv infers column types from a sample and prints the parsed spec, making type problems visible early. It also handles encodings and missing-value tokens cleanly via na = argument. Base read.csv historically forced stringsAsFactors = TRUE (before R 4.0), a frequent source of bugs.
codeExample:
# Base R
df1 <- read.csv("data.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# readr (tidyverse) -> tibble, faster, clearer
library(readr)
df2 <- read_csv(
  "data.csv",
  col_types = cols(
    id = col_integer(),
    date = col_date(format = "%Y-%m-%d")
  ),
  na = c("", "NA", "NULL")
)

# Fastest for big files
# df3 <- data.table::fread("data.csv")
bestPractices: Specify col_types to avoid surprises with readr
Use fread() for very large files
commonMistakes: Relying on automatic factor conversion from old read.csv
interviewTips: Note read_csv returns a tibble and does not auto-create factors

title: Detecting and imputing missing data
question: How do you detect the extent of missing data and impute it in R?
answer: Detect missingness with is.na() combined with colSums() to count NAs per column, or complete.cases() to find fully observed rows. Impute by replacing NAs with a statistic like the mean/median, using tidyr::replace_na(), dplyr::coalesce(), or dedicated packages such as mice for multiple imputation.
explanation: Simple mean/median imputation is quick but distorts variance and can bias analysis, so it should be used cautiously. Multiple imputation (mice) models the missingness and produces several completed datasets, propagating uncertainty. Always understand whether data is missing completely at random before choosing a strategy.
codeExample:
df <- data.frame(a = c(1, NA, 3), b = c(NA, 5, 6))

colSums(is.na(df))          # NAs per column
mean(complete.cases(df))    # fraction of complete rows

# Median imputation
impute_median <- function(x) {
  x[is.na(x)] <- median(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  x
}
df$a <- impute_median(df$a)

# tidyverse
library(tidyr)
df |> replace_na(list(b = 0))
bestPractices: Quantify missingness before imputing
Prefer multiple imputation (mice) for inferential work
commonMistakes: Blind mean imputation that shrinks variance and biases estimates
interviewTips: Mention missing-data mechanisms (MCAR, MAR, MNAR)

title: Statistical functions in R
question: What built-in statistical functions and distributions does R provide?
answer: R ships with a rich statistical toolkit: summary statistics like mean(), median(), sd(), var(), quantile(), and cor(); modelling functions like lm() and glm(); hypothesis tests like t.test() and chisq.test(); and a consistent family of distribution functions using d/p/q/r prefixes (density, cumulative probability, quantile, random generation).
explanation: The distribution naming convention is systematic: for the normal distribution, dnorm gives density, pnorm the CDF, qnorm the quantile (inverse CDF), and rnorm draws random samples. The same d/p/q/r pattern applies to binom, pois, unif, and many others, making the API predictable.
codeExample:
x <- c(4, 8, 15, 16, 23, 42)
mean(x); median(x); sd(x)
quantile(x, probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75))

# Distribution functions (d/p/q/r)
dnorm(0)              # density at 0
pnorm(1.96)           # ~0.975 CDF
qnorm(0.975)          # ~1.96 quantile
set.seed(1)
rnorm(3, mean = 10, sd = 2)   # random draws

# Linear model
fit <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)
summary(fit)$coefficients
bestPractices: Set a seed for reproducible random generation
Learn the d/p/q/r convention once and reuse everywhere
commonMistakes: Confusing pnorm (CDF) with qnorm (inverse CDF)
interviewTips: Explain the d/p/q/r prefix pattern for distributions

title: Reshaping with tidyr
question: How do pivot_longer and pivot_wider reshape data in R?
answer: tidyr::pivot_longer() converts wide data into long (tidy) format by stacking multiple columns into key-value pairs, while pivot_wider() does the reverse, spreading a key column's values into multiple columns. Long format, where each variable is a column and each observation a row, is the shape most tidyverse tools expect.
explanation: pivot_longer takes the columns to melt, a names_to for the new key column, and a values_to for the values. pivot_wider takes names_from and values_from. They replaced the older gather()/spread() functions with clearer, more powerful arguments.
codeExample:
library(tidyr)

wide <- data.frame(
  id = 1:2,
  q1 = c(10, 20),
  q2 = c(30, 40)
)

long <- wide |> pivot_longer(
  cols = c(q1, q2),
  names_to = "quarter",
  values_to = "sales"
)

back <- long |> pivot_wider(
  names_from = quarter,
  values_from = sales
)
bestPractices: Store data in long/tidy form for most tidyverse workflows
Use pivot_longer/pivot_wider over deprecated gather/spread
commonMistakes: Reaching for the older gather()/spread() functions
interviewTips: Define 'tidy data': one variable per column, one observation per row

title: ggplot2 grammar of graphics
question: What are the core building blocks of a ggplot2 plot?
answer: ggplot2 implements the grammar of graphics. A plot is built by combining a data set, aesthetic mappings via aes() that link variables to visual properties, and one or more geoms (geometric layers) added with +. Optional components include scales, faceting, statistical transformations, coordinate systems, and themes.
explanation: You start with ggplot(data, aes(...)) to declare the data and mappings, then add layers such as geom_point() or geom_line(). Because layers are added with +, plots are highly composable. facet_wrap() splits data into small multiples, and themes control non-data ink like fonts and gridlines.
codeExample:
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ gear) +
  labs(
    title = "Fuel efficiency vs weight",
    x = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
    y = "Miles per gallon",
    color = "Cylinders"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
bestPractices: Map variables inside aes(), set constants outside it
Layer geoms and themes with + for composability
commonMistakes: Putting a constant color inside aes(), creating a spurious legend
interviewTips: Name the grammar components: data, aesthetics, geoms, scales, facets

title: Copy-on-modify semantics
question: How does R's copy-on-modify behavior affect memory and performance?
answer: R objects use copy-on-modify semantics: multiple bindings can share the same underlying memory, and R only makes a copy when one of them is modified. This gives value semantics (assignment appears to copy) while avoiding needless duplication until a change actually occurs. Environments and R6 objects are the exceptions, using reference semantics.
explanation: You can inspect this with tracemem() and lobstr::obj_addr(). A common performance trap is modifying a column of a large data frame inside a loop, which can trigger repeated copies. Vectorized operations, preallocation, and packages like data.table (which modifies in place) avoid this overhead.
codeExample:
x <- c(1, 2, 3)
y <- x            # shares memory, no copy yet

tracemem(x)
y[1] <- 99        # now a copy is made for y
# tracemem[0x...]: copy triggered here

untracemem(x)

# Trap: growing/modifying in a loop copies repeatedly
# Prefer vectorized ops or preallocation
n <- 1e4
res <- numeric(n)          # preallocate
for (i in seq_len(n)) res[i] <- i^2
bestPractices: Preallocate vectors instead of growing them
Use data.table for in-place modification of large data
commonMistakes: Modifying large objects in loops, causing repeated copies
interviewTips: Mention tracemem() and that environments use reference semantics
